index.html
Vibe Coding for Data Analysis Preface Vibe Coding vs. Vibe Analysis Why Vibe Analysis? Best Practices for the Vibe Workflow

Vibe Coding for Data Analysis Rishabh 2026-04-24 Preface Welcome to Vibe Coding for Data Analysis. “Vibe coding” is a somewhat colloquial term for a profound shift in software development and data science: the practice of using natural language models to translate intent directly into executable code. It represents moving away from manual syntax reproduction and towards becoming an architect of analysis. If you spend more time trying to remember the exact arguments of dplyr::pivot_longer() or the aesthetics of a ggplot2 chart than thinking about the insights your data contains, this book is for you. Vibe Coding vs. Vibe Analysis While “vibe coding” generally refers to building software tools or applications through natural language interactions with AI, Vibe Analysis applies this paradigm specifically to data science and analytics. Vibe coding might focus on creating a functioning React component or a Python backend service, prioritizing whether the code compiles and runs. Vibe Analysis, however, holds a higher standard: statistical correctness, reproducibility, and rigorous logic. A vibe-coded web app might look slightly off but still work; a vibe-analysed dataset with a silent hallucination in an aggregation step will lead to fundamentally wrong business or research conclusions. Why Vibe Analysis? In traditional data analysis, the bottleneck is often the translation of a statistical idea into programming syntax. You have a question (“How do sales compare across regions over the last three quarters?”), but you must spend time writing boilerplate code to group, summarize, and plot the data. With AI assistants—specifically GitHub Copilot, the focus of this book—you can write the question in plain English as a comment, and the assistant will synthesize the code. Your role changes: From typist to director: You set the overarching strategy. From debugger to auditor: You review and validate the logic the AI proposes. From memorizer to thinker: You focus on research design and statistical validity rather than syntax. Best Practices for the Vibe Workflow To get the most out of GitHub Copilot in your data analysis workflow, it is crucial to adopt a few core practices: Plan, Act, Review: Slice your analytical tasks into smaller, manageable steps. Do not ask for a full dashboard immediately. Ask for data import, then cleaning, then visualization. Context is King: The AI needs to see your data structure. Print the head() or str() of your data frame so Copilot understands the variable names and types. Human-in-the-Loop: Always verify intermediate steps. A model might confidently generate code that looks correct but performs an aggregation incorrectly. Create Persistent Context: Document your data assumptions in a PROJECT.md file or at the top of your script to keep Copilot aligned with your goals. In the subsequent chapters, we’ll dive right into setting up your environment, writing effective prompts for data wrangling, and iterating on visualizations entirely through conversation.
getting-started.html
Chapter 1 Getting Started 1.1 Choosing Your IDE 1.2 Activating GitHub Copilot 1.3 Project-Level Copilot Instructions 1.4 Writing Your First Prompt

Chapter 1 Getting Started To master vibe coding in R, your environment needs to be properly set up. You need a frictionless way to communicate with your AI pair programmer so that writing a prompt feels as fluid as writing native R code. 1.1 Choosing Your IDE While standard RStudio (Posit) is the classic choice for R users, the advent of AI assistants has made Visual Studio Code (VS Code) a highly competitive alternative. RStudio: Recent versions of RStudio include native integration with GitHub Copilot. Setup is as straightforward as checking an option in the Global Options menu. The interface is optimized specifically for R, which handles environments and plotting seamlessly. VS Code: Provides deeper customization and often earlier access to the most advanced Copilot features (like Copilot Chat). The vscode-R extension has made VS Code a robust R IDE. For the purpose of this book, we assume you are using GitHub Copilot as your assistant, integrated into whichever IDE you prefer. 1.2 Activating GitHub Copilot Once you have your IDE set up, you’ll need an active GitHub Copilot subscription. In RStudio: 1. Go to Tools -> Global Options -> Copilot. 2. Check “Enable GitHub Copilot”. 3. Sign in to your GitHub account when prompted. In VS Code: 1. Install the “GitHub Copilot” extension. 2. Install the “GitHub Copilot Chat” extension for interactive conversations. 3. Sign in to GitHub through the bottom-left account icon. 1.3 Project-Level Copilot Instructions To write code in a consistent style across your project, you can provide general guidance to GitHub Copilot by creating an instructions file in your workspace. For instance, in VS Code or GitHub Copilot, you can specify custom instructions in a file like .github/copilot-instructions.md. This ensures that the AI defaults to your preferred libraries and formatting styles (such as strongly preferring tidyverse over base R). Here is an example of what that markdown file might contain to enforce consistent R and Python code styles: # General Guidance - Prioritize readability and maintainability. - Add comments explaining *why* something is done, not just *what*. # R Specific Guidelines - ALWAYS use `tidyverse` (e.g., `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`, `readr`) instead of base R functions. For example, use `read_csv()` rather than `read.csv()`. - Use the pipe operator `%>%` (or `|>`) to chain functions sequentially. - Use `snake_case` for all variable and function names. # Python Specific Guidelines - Use `pandas` for data manipulation, aiming for vectorized operations rather than base loops. - Use `snake_case` for variables and functions, and `PascalCase` for classes. - Provide type hints for all function arguments and return types. By adding these project-level instructions, Copilot will stay consistent with your team’s best practices out of the box, significantly reducing manual refactoring later. 1.4 Writing Your First Prompt With Copilot active, let’s write our first vibe code. Open a new R script. Let’s say you want to generate a random dataset to practice with. Instead of trying to remember the exact syntax for creating a data frame with random normal distributions, you simply write a comment describing what you want: # Create a data frame 'sales_data' with 100 rows. # Columns: 'date' (random days in 2023), 'region' (North, South, East, West), and 'revenue' (random normal dist, mean 500) Press Enter and wait a second. Copilot will synthesize the code in gray text (ghost text). If it looks correct, press Tab to accept it! # Create a data frame 'sales_data' with 100 rows. # Columns: 'date' (random days in 2023), 'region' (North, South, East, West), and 'revenue' (random normal dist, mean 500) sales_data <- data.frame( date = sample(seq(as.Date('2023/01/01'), as.Date('2023/12/31'), by="day"), 100, replace = TRUE), region = sample(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), 100, replace = TRUE), revenue = rnorm(100, mean = 500, sd = 100) ) You’ve just completed your first vibe-coding task! You expressed the intent, and Copilot provided the syntax. In the next chapter, we’ll dive into the detailed workflow for more complex data manipulation.
the-vibe-workflow.html

Chapter 2 The Vibe Workflow 2.1 Applying the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) 2.2 The “Plan, Act, Review” Cycle 2.3 Prioritizing Context 2.4 Prompting for Data Manipulation (dplyr) 2.5 Adding Iterative Chaining

Chapter 2 The Vibe Workflow 2.1 Applying the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) In traditional Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC), teams move through distinct phases: requirements gathering, design, implementation, testing, and deployment. To ensure Vibe Analysis is high-quality and rigorous, we must adopt an SDLC mindset for our data projects. Instead of treating data analysis as an ad-hoc script, treat it as a software product. The AI writes the code, but you enforce the lifecycle: writing clear requirements (prompts), enforcing code reviews (auditing AI outputs), and rigorous testing (data validation and sanity checks). This structured approach minimizes hallucinations and ensures that the heavily automated coding process yields reliable, production-ready analytical pipelines. When analyzing data, standard manual coding requires translating every logical step into its corresponding R function (e.g., “I need group_by() then summarize()”). Vibe coding allows you to stay at the conceptual level, but only if you provide effective prompts and maintain a strong workflow. 2.2 The “Plan, Act, Review” Cycle Vibe coding is not magic; you are managing a very fast, sometimes overconfident junior analyst. You should adopt a “Plan, Act, Review” cycle: Plan: Break down the analysis into steps. Don’t ask for a final predictive model from a raw dataset in one prompt. Act: Write a clear prompt for the first step. Review: Examine the generated code. Does it use the libraries you want? Run the code and use glimpse() or head() to verify the result before moving to the next step. 2.3 Prioritizing Context GitHub Copilot is a context engine. It reads the files you have open and the code preceding your cursor. If Copilot doesn’t know what columns your dataset has, its suggestions will be educated guesses (i.e., hallucinations). Best Practice: Show, don’t tell. Let Copilot “read” your data structure by doing this at the top of your script: library(dplyr) # Assume we loaded 'sales_data' earlier. # str(sales_data) # 'data.frame': 100 obs. of 3 variables: # $ date : Date, format: "2023-04-12" "2023-08-22" ... # $ region : chr "North" "South" "East" "West" ... # $ revenue: num 480.5 512.3 640.1 498.2 ... By pasting the str() or glimpse() output as a comment, Copilot now knows exactly what columns exist. 2.4 Prompting for Data Manipulation (dplyr) When you want to wrangle data, use intent-based prompts that specify the library if you have a preference. # Using dplyr, calculate the total revenue and average revenue per region for Q1 2023. Copilot will read the context (your str output) and generate: q1_summary <- sales_data %>% filter(date >= "2023-01-01" & date <= "2023-03-31") %>% group_by(region) %>% summarize( total_rev = sum(revenue), avg_rev = mean(revenue) ) 2.5 Adding Iterative Chaining If the result is almost what you want, but you forgot a detail, just add another comment to chain onto the pipeline: # Now arrange the previous result in descending order by total_rev q1_summary <- q1_summary %>% arrange(desc(total_rev)) By working in chunks, reviewing the intermediate states, and feeding that state back as context, you maintain total control over your data analysis while typing a fraction of the code.
visualizing-with-vibes.html
Chapter 3 Visualizing with Vibes 3.1 Prompting for Plots 3.2 Iterating on the Design 3.3 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Chapter 3 Visualizing with Vibes Data visualization is arguably where vibe coding shines the brightest. The ggplot2 package is incredibly powerful, but its granular grammar of graphics (themes, scales, geoms, facetting) requires remembering hundreds of specific arguments. Finding the right syntax to rotate x-axis labels by 45 degrees or move the legend to the bottom often leads analysts to Google. With GitHub Copilot, you simply ask for the aesthetic. 3.1 Prompting for Plots Let’s assume we want to visualize the summary data we created in the last chapter. If you just write # Plot the data, Copilot might give you a standard, ugly base R plot. You must be an architect and give specific design orders. library(ggplot2) # Create a bar chart showing total revenue by region using the q1_summary data frame. # Specifics: # - Reorder the bars so the highest revenue is first. # - Fill the bars with a steelblue color. # - Use a minimal theme and remove the grid lines. # - Add a title "Q1 2023 Revenue by Region" and remove the x-axis label. Copilot will likely generate a perfectly structured ggplot pipeline: ggplot(q1_summary, aes(x = reorder(region, -total_rev), y = total_rev)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.title.x = element_blank() ) + labs( title = "Q1 2023 Revenue by Region", y = "Total Revenue" ) 3.2 Iterating on the Design Often, the first plot is roughly correct, but you spot a visual flaw. Instead of digging into the theme() documentation, you keep vibe coding. Imagine the revenue numbers are in the tens of thousands and look messy (e.g., 45000). # Update the previous plot to format the y-axis labels as dollars with commas (using the scales package). # Also, add comma-formatted data labels on top of each bar. The AI updates the code: library(scales) ggplot(q1_summary, aes(x = reorder(region, -total_rev), y = total_rev)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + geom_text(aes(label = dollar(total_rev)), vjust = -0.5) + scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar) + # The AI handles the scales package syntax theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.title.x = element_blank() ) + labs( title = "Q1 2023 Revenue by Region", y = "Total Revenue" ) 3.3 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) You can use vibe coding to rapid-fire through different visual hypotheses during EDA. # Create a scatter plot of revenue vs date from sales_data. # Add a loess trend line in red. # Facet the plot by region. By conversationalizing the visualization step, you reduce the friction between a question (“Is revenue trending up differently in the South?”) and the visual answer.
modeling-and-machine-learning.html
Chapter 4 Modeling and Machine Learning 4.1 Prompting for Tidymodels 4.2 Reviewing the Architecture 4.3 Evaluating the Model

Chapter 4 Modeling and Machine Learning While data cleaning and visualization benefit greatly from vibe coding, machine learning requires much more caution. When prompting for statistical models, the risk of “hallucinations” (the AI confidently generating statistically invalid methods or misinterpreting data) increases significantly. The AI does not inherently understand the assumptions of linear regression or the concept of data leakage. You must be highly explicit about your research design. 4.1 Prompting for Tidymodels The tidymodels framework in R provides a structured, modern way to do machine learning, but its syntax can be verbose (recipes, specs, workflows). Copilot handles this verbosity well. Start by defining the split and the recipe clearly: library(tidymodels) # Create a data split for sales_data: 80% training, 20% testing. # Set the seed to 123 for reproducibility. set.seed(123) sales_split <- initial_split(sales_data, prop = 0.8) sales_train <- training(sales_split) sales_test <- testing(sales_split) Now, construct the recipe. Explain the exact transformations you want: # Create a tidymodels recipe predicting 'revenue' using all other variables. # - Extract the month and day of the week from the 'date' column. # - Remove the original 'date' column. # - Dummy encode all nominal predictors. 4.2 Reviewing the Architecture This is where the Review step of the “Plan, Act, Review” workflow is critical. Read the generated recipe. Did it dummy encode correctly? Did it handle missing values as you intended? If you blindly accept ML code from Copilot, you will likely encounter errors when you fit() the model or, worse, generate an invalid predictive model that looks fine on the surface. 4.3 Evaluating the Model Once you have fitted a workflow, you can ask Copilot to generate the evaluation metrics, which saves you from remembering the exact yardstick functions: # Assuming 'rf_fit' is our fitted tidymodels workflow. # Generate predictions on the sales_test data. # Calculate RMSE and RSQ for the predictions. By constraining Copilot to write the plumbing (the tidymodels syntax) while you define the architecture (the features, the model type, the validation strategy), you balance speed with statistical rigor.
troubleshooting.html
Chapter 5 Troubleshooting 5.1 Types of Errors 5.2 The Chat is Your Debugger 5.3 The “Rubber Duck” Technique

Chapter 5 Troubleshooting Vibe coding is not a perfect process. You will encounter errors. Variables will be misspelled, arguments will be deprecated, and the AI will sometimes suggest functions from packages you haven’t loaded. Knowing how to troubleshoot AI-generated code is a fundamental skill of the vibe coding workflow. 5.1 Types of Errors Syntax Errors: Missing parentheses or commas. Copilot is usually very good at avoiding these, but they happen if you edit the code manually after generation. Logic Errors: The code runs perfectly, but it groups by the wrong column or joins the wrong tables. These are the most dangerous. You must validate intermediate states. Hallucinated Packages/Functions: Copilot might suggest a function like calculate_revenue() thinking it represents a built-in function, when in reality it doesn’t exist. 5.2 The Chat is Your Debugger When you use GitHub Copilot, you are not limited to inline ghost text. If you are using RStudio’s Copilot integration or VS Code with Copilot Chat, you have a conversational debugger. If an error is thrown in your console: Error in `mutate()`: ℹ In argument: `sales = as.numeric(revenue)`. Caused by error: ! object 'revenue' not found Do not spend 15 minutes staring at the code trying to find the typo. Simply copy the error message, highlight the chunk of code that caused it, and paste it into Copilot Chat: “I’m getting this error: [paste error]. Here is my data str(): [paste str]. Fix the code.” Copilot will immediately recognize that revenue is misspelled in your data frame, or that you forgot to load the dataset, or that it generated the wrong column name earlier. 5.3 The “Rubber Duck” Technique Sometimes, the logic is just complex. If you are writing a custom simulation or a massive nested loop, Copilot might get confused. When the code gets messy, delete it. Go back to plain English. Write a multi-line comment essentially talking to the AI (or a rubber duck) about what you are trying to achieve, step-by-step. Let it generate the code one tiny block at a time. Forcing the AI—and yourself—to slow down and explain the logic often solves the problem.
case-studies-what-can-go-wrong.html
Chapter 6 Case Studies: What Can Go Wrong? 6.1 The Illusion of Productivity 6.2 Case Study 1: The “Happy Path” Data Join 6.3 Case Study 2: Hallucinated Business Logic 6.4 The Velocity Gap

Chapter 6 Case Studies: What Can Go Wrong? Vibe coding can act as a force multiplier, but blindly trusting AI to generate complex analytical logic exposes you to several well-documented risks. In this chapter, we explore realistic case studies and research demonstrating what happens when the “Review” phase of the vibe-coding workflow is skipped. 6.1 The Illusion of Productivity A significant psychological trap of vibe coding is the “Illusion of Productivity.” When you use GitHub Copilot, you feel extremely productive because lines of code appear on your screen instantaneously. However, a recent industry study found an interesting contradiction: while developers subjectively felt 20% faster when using AI tools, they were actually 19% slower when executing complex tasks compared to traditional coding methods. Why? Because generating the code took seconds, but identifying the subtle logical flaws the AI introduced took hours. Reading and debugging unfamiliar, AI-generated code is often harder than writing it yourself. 6.2 Case Study 1: The “Happy Path” Data Join The Context: An analyst was tasked with combining two datasets: a primary customer_transactions table and a regional_metadata table. They vibe-coded the following prompt: # Join the transaction data with the metadata to get the region for each customer. What Copilot Generated: combined_data <- customer_transactions %>% inner_join(regional_metadata, by = "customer_id") The Failure: The AI assumed an inner_join was appropriate. However, the regional_metadata table was incomplete. Because an inner_join drops unmatched rows, the analyst silently lost 15% of their transaction data. The code ran without any R errors, looking perfectly successful on the “happy path.” The Fix: The prompt lacking context caused the error. The analyst should have specified a left_join and added a validation step to check for dropped rows. 6.3 Case Study 2: Hallucinated Business Logic The Context: A data scientist wanted to calculate the churn rate for a SaaS product. # Calculate the monthly churn rate for the platform. What Copilot Generated: churn_summary <- user_data %>% group_by(month) %>% summarize( total_users = n(), churned_users = sum(status == "cancelled"), churn_rate = churned_users / total_users ) The Failure: This code looks mathematically flawless. However, the business logic locally defined a “churned user” as someone who cancelled and didn’t reactivate within 14 days. The AI had no way of knowing this specific domain context. It relied on its training data to generate a generic, plausible definition of churn. The reporting was fundamentally wrong. 6.4 The Velocity Gap These logic failures contribute to what security researchers call the “Velocity Gap.” Because AI can pump out code incredibly fast, an analyst can generate 500 lines of data wrangling, feature engineering, and modeling scripts in an hour. This rapid production easily overwhelms existing peer review and QA processes. Critical vulnerabilities (such as exposed PII in logs or logically faulty data transformations) are created faster than human reviewers can detect them. To combat this, you cannot rely entirely on traditional review methods. In the next chapter, we look at how to formally audit AI-generated code.
auditing-ai-code-review-templates.html
Chapter 7 Auditing AI: Code Review Templates 7.1 The “Two-Gate” Mental Model 7.2 Demanding Evidence 7.3 The AI Code Review Template

Chapter 7 Auditing AI: Code Review Templates Because AI models predict plausible code rather than deeply “understanding” business requirements, any output generated by GitHub Copilot must be treated as untrusted by default. Reviewing AI-generated code requires a fundamental shift in mindset. You must review the behavior, not just the syntax. AI code is usually well-formatted, which can mask dangerous logic. 7.1 The “Two-Gate” Mental Model When reviewing a script or a Pull Request heavily assisted by AI, separate your evaluation into two distinct gates: The Run Gate (Safety): Before you even assess if the code is logically correct, you must evaluate its impact alias “blast radius.” Does this code interact with a database? Does it write files to disk? Could it alter external states? Do not execute the code locally until you have cleared it through the Run Gate. The Ship Gate (Quality): Once deemed safe to run, review the logic, the edge cases, and whether it solves the actual business requirement. 7.2 Demanding Evidence A common mistake is reading AI code, seeing it has standard comments, and thinking “It looks like it handles null values.” Do not accept appearance as proof. Demand evidence. If Copilot writes a complex tidyr::pivot_wider() and aggregation pipeline, you or the reviewer must run unit tests or manual verification scripts showing what happens when the data contains NAs. 7.3 The AI Code Review Template To ensure these best practices are embedded in your team’s workflow, we recommend using a standardized review template. You can copy the following checklist into your Pull Request templates on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. 7.3.1 Pull Request Review: AI-Assisted Code 1. Safety & Impact Analysis (The “Run Gate”) - [ ] Blast Radius: Does this code modify persistent data, write to a database, or alter systems (e.g., dbWriteTable in R)? - [ ] Data Access: Does this script process sensitive data or PII? If so, does it log or expose it unnecessarily? - [ ] Dependencies: Have any new CRAN or GitHub packages been introduced by the AI? Are they vetted? 2. Functional Correctness (The “Ship Gate”) - [ ] Context vs Assumption: Does this code solve our specific problem, or did the AI hallucinate a simpler problem? Did it use correct business logic for metrics (e.g., how we calculate Revenue)? - [ ] Edge Cases Handle: How does this code handle missing values (NA), empty data frames, or duplicate records? - [ ] Data Loss: Are there any inner_joins or filter steps that silently drop expected data? 3. Architecture & Traceability - [ ] Reinvention: Did the AI reinvent functionality that already exists in our internal packages or utility scripts? - [ ] Evidence: Has the submitter provided unit test results or output logs showing the execution works for boundary cases? Reviewer Verdict: - [ ] Approve (Changes are safe and logically sound) - [ ] Request Changes (Logic gaps, edge cases missed, or unnecessary abstraction) - Confidence Score (1-5): ______ (How confident are you in the AI’s logic?) By enforcing this structure, you close the Velocity Gap. You ensure that the speed of vibe coding does not come at the expense of accuracy and reliability in your analytical output.
conclusion.html
Chapter 8 Conclusion 8.1 Becoming an Architect

Chapter 8 Conclusion Vibe coding is fundamentally changing what it means to be a data analyst or data scientist. By abstracting away the syntax, GitHub Copilot allows us to operate closer to the speed of thought. However, that speed is dangerous without discipline. 8.1 Becoming an Architect As you adopt this workflow, remember that the AI is your fast-typing assistant. Keep the following principles in mind: Never surrender the logic. You are responsible for the statistical validity of the analysis. If you don’t know what a block of code does, don’t run it. Use Copilot Chat to say, “Explain this code to me line by line.” Context is everything. The better you describe your data and your intent, the better the AI’s output will be. Validate continuously. Check your row counts after joins. Check your distributions after mutations. Embrace the conversational workflow. When you hit an error, don’t revert to manual debugging immediately. Paste the error into the chat and let the assistant figure it out. The goal of learning R is no longer to be a walking syntax dictionary. The goal is to understand how to manipulate data conceptually so that you can effectively guide your AI to write the syntax for you. Welcome to the future of data analysis. Happy vibe coding.
